title: 第 22 節
en: One can hardly understand how such beings can have been tolerated, unless we admit a faint recollection of their original meaning, at all events among the more cultivated classes, whether in Greece, in India, or in Finland.

Heroes parallel with Gods. But the question of the relation of heroes or demigods to the gods, which was so fully discussed by Grimm, Schwartz, and others, admits of another solution also. There must have been cases where, from the very beginning, the exploits of these deities, more particularly of solar deities, were related in so homely and so realistic a fashion that, from the very first, the chief agents in them could never have been taken for immortals, but must have assumed at once the character of less divine and almost human beings, or at all events of beings but little above the measure of ordinary mortals. These so-called demigods or heroes, such as Herakles, often share certain epithets in common with their relatives among the gods. They are often called the children of divine Io8 HEROES PARALLEL WITH GODS. [chap.

fathers and of human mothers. They even receive a recognised worship, and are sometimes allowed in the end to join the company of the gods. This shows once more why we need not with Grimm take all heroes of popular tales as corruptions of the gods whose character they share, but may accept some of them at least, as sprung from the same source with the gods, only turned from the first into a different channel.
Helena both Goddess and Heroine.

It is well known, for instance, that Helena was a goddess, and had her own temples and worship in Greece. But it does not follow that this goddess became afterwards Helen, whether carried off by Theseus to Aphidnae, or by Paris to Troy.

Such a process would be difficult to understand, while it becomes intelligible as soon as we admit a cluster of legends springing up about the name of Helena, some of them combining to form the image of a goddess, others the image of a heroine. It does not signify at this point of our inquiry whether we assign to the name of Helena the original meaning of Dawn (Sarama) or of Moon (Selene). It suffices, if we want to account for the co-existence of a goddess and of a heroine Helena, that we should remember how her extremely human characteristics could have been gathered up in the beautiful heroine only, while her superhuman qualities fitted her for divine honours such as she certainly received in ancient Greece 1. In this way the objections raised by Schwartz against Grimm's system may be re- 1 Th. Heicks, De Helena Dea, Sigmaringen, 1863.
Il] HELENA BOTH GODDESS AND HEEOINE. 109 moved, and yet the process of the simultaneous origin of gods and heroes from similar antecedents be fully accounted for.

Dr. Halm on Marchen. This question has been very fully discussed by Hahn in his important work, Sagwissenschaftliche Studien. On page 51 he writes: 'We are forced to admit that the traditions produced by the most ancient races are not worked up in their completeness in what we possess of their mythology of gods and heroes. A considerable portion of these primordial thoughts has been preserved to the present day, nay, in spite of its enormous age we see it growing with undiminished vigour in the popular mind, and powerfully reacting upon it. This is proved by the existence of the Marchen, and the palpable relationship of the Miirchen among people of the same origin.' After pointing out the difficulties which stand in the way of admitting a mere borrowing of Marchen by one race from another, Dr. Hahn shows that a careful analysis of these popular stories discloses, as their original contents, the same natural phenomena which supplied the material of the mythical stories of gods and heroes, clothed in a more homely form. The origin of these Marchen, which we find to the present day in the different branches of the Aryan family of speech, is referred by him to a period preceding the Aryan separation. In all this he may be perfectly right, but he is wrong when he declines to take into account the historical and much later migration of fables from India to Europe, which Benfey has proved by evidence which cannot be IIO DR. HAHN ON MAECHEN. [chap.

questioned. If we remove all that has been shown to have been imported in historical times from India to Europe from station to station, from Sanskrit to Pahlavi, to Arabic, Greek, Hebrew, Latin, &c, there remains still plenty of popular tradition that has to be accounted for in other ways. Benfey's facts, it seems to me, are unassailable, and the results established by Hahn are not in any conflict with Benfey's system. The process is exactly the same as when, by the side of a large number of common Aryan words, we find in English a class of foreign names exported in historical times from India or Persia to the British Isles, or from Greek or Latin to Gothic. The two facts are perfectly compatible, nay, it happens but seldom that we are left in doubt whether any of these fables form part of the common Aryan heirloom or are imports of a later time.
Beginnings of Mythology Lost.

We should never forget how limited our knowledge of ancient popular tradition really is, even in the case of Hindus and Greeks, and how the earliest chapters of mythology are lost to us for ever. To the Greeks the Homeric poems were the most distant background of their mythology and religion, nay, of their history also ; to us they are a beautifully painted curtain which must be lifted before we can hope to see the earliest acts of the drama of mythology, or to recognise the original actors and the natural scenery by which they were surrounded.

How true are the words of Kekule in his Entstehung der Gotterideale, 1877, when he describes Greek mythology as a mere fragment taken from Il] BEGINNINGS OF MYTHOLOGY LOST. Ill the worlds of metaphor and dreams piled one upon the other by the hand of man, or as one page torn from the great picture-book of nature, for which nothing is too small, nothing too sublime.

' Then,' he continues, ' as soon as one of these pictures ceases to be intelligible to later generations, and becomes a mere name, a new metaphor, a new form, a new poem rises from the well of language and poetry to represent the same natural phenomenon in its coming and going, till that living fountain is wellnigh dried up, though it never dries up altogether, always sending up new figures, only less powerful than before, till of the unforgotten, mighty, primeval notes and metaphors of nature's own poetry nothing remains but the names and persons of gods and heroes, with their stories which send forth fresh shoots without ceasing.'
Schelling.

It was Schelling, I believe, who was the first to complain of the ' shallowness of any admiration for Homer which is not founded on a perception of the remote past, left behind (iiberwunden) by his creations.' It was only after Schelling's death that by means of Comparative Philology and Comparative Mythology it became possible to lift, to a certain extent, the curtain which, as he well saw, divided the Homeric present from the Homeric past. With every year we have learnt more and more how very modern the Homeric poems really are, I mean, how much they presuppose, and how much of the rich growth of religious and mythological folklore they leave unnoticed. If the Iliad gives us a small fragment only of the Siege of Troy, both Iliad and Odyssey give us a still smaller fragment only SCHELLING. [chap.
of the vast treasure of the widely scattered myths, traditions, legends, and superstitions of the Greek people in its numerous branches.

And what applies to Greek applies to all mythologies, even to that of the Veda, though here, better than anywhere else, we are sometimes allowed to watch the very process of fermentation which always precedes the birth of real mythology.
The Original Elements of Mythology.

From all that has been learnt, partly from a study of the Veda, partly also from a scholarlike analysis of remnants of ancient mythology among other races, the conclusions reached, and now most generally adopted with regard to the origin of mythology in general, may be summed up as follows.

The process by which what are called the gods, whether Devas, or ©eot, or Dii, or tivar, were originally called into being, was perfectly natural, nay, it was inevitable. We ourselves, living on the vast accumulated wealth of language, i. e. thought, are enabled to speak of natural forces which produce a thunderstorm, with its lightnings and showers ; but what could the ancients have said ? They had no word, no thought, for forces in our sense of the word. And perhaps it was fortunate that they had not, for what do we ourselves mean by forces in their substantial character? They are, to repeat once more the words of R. von Mayer, no better than the gods of Greece. If the ancient Greeks or the Aryas of India began to ask, whence came rain and lightning, whence sprang hail and snow, heat and cold, day and night, coming and going in regular or irregular succession, they could only Il] OEIGINAL ELEMENTS OF MYTHOLOGY. 113 speak of agents and workers, as they spoke of agents and workers who had ploughed the land, forged the iron, or built a hut. And this arose not only from a necessity of thought, but at the same time from a necessity of language. If they wished to form the first names for the wind, or the fire, or the sun by names such as alone their language could produce, they had to make use of the same radical elements from which all their words had been derived, i.e. the so-called roots, their earliest predicates, their earliest abstractions, their earliest general terms. Without general terms there can be no names, except imitations like cuckoo or bow-wow. As they called a potter a kneader or shaper, from a root dih, to knead, to shape, and a butcher a clissecter, samitri, from sam, used in the sense of preparing or making ready, they called the wind a blower, Vayu, from va, to blow; they called the sun Savitrz, from su, to stir, the cloud Megha, from mih, to moisten, or Par^anya, from a root meaning to sprinkle, preserved in a-spergo1. By creating these names they created their Devas, whose Devahood, that is whose brightness, and afterwards divinity, was but the general complement of their physical activity.

If the first idea of an object arose, as Noire has shown, from the consciousness of an opus operatum, a cave dug out or a flint polished, the idea of cause was realised for the first time in the consciousness of an act, of force exercised by man himself, and in the recognition of the Devas, or what we call the forces of nature, the nomina agentis of mythology. Most of 1 See M. M., India, &c, p. 227 seq. VOL. I. I 114 ORIGINAL ELEMENTS OF MYTHOLOGY. [chap.

the Aryan roots expressed actions, and hence the names given to the great phenomena or activities of nature could not be anything but what they are, nomina agentis. I need not enter once more into the question of the origin of roots, the origin of origins, so to say, or explain once more why roots were naturally expressive of actions, as these questions have been fully treated in my Science of Thought. Suffice it to state that no attempt at going beyond or analysing these roots either phonetically or logically has hitherto led to any results likely to benefit the student of mythology, though as a philosophical problem the origin of roots will always continue to exercise its charm on human curiosity. To say that these roots are emotional is saying no more than that all the impressions of our senses are emotional, and cannot be anything else.

To us, however, these roots are historical monuments, more ancient than any human monuments on the face of the whole earth. Being roots they could never have existed by themselves, but they were that without which no words could have existed. To us they are of course abstractions, gathered from the various words in which they occur. But in order to occur in those various words they had to be something real and independent, just as the threads, before they could be woven into any kind of tissue, had to exist in the hands of the weavers. When these roots had once been used for forming names of objects that could be comprehended under them, the Aryan speakers found themselves in possession of such words as Agni, Indra, Ushas, and all the rest. Agni, the fire, meant originally no more than the agile, the swift mover, Vahni meant much the Il] ORIGINAL ELEMENTS OF MYTHOLOGY. II5 same, so did Bhura?zyu and other names of fire. Indra meant the moistener, Ushas, the shiner. Besides expressing, however, these general activities, these names had a more definite purpose also, and they reminded those who used them of many things besides the simple acts expressed by their roots. Agni to the minds and memories of those who had formed that name was not only the quivering and flashing light, or the source of warmth and light on the hearth of every house, he was also the devourer of forests, the fatal lightning bursting from the clouds, the fiery ball rising every day from the ocean and vanishing again in the waters which had given him birth. He was remembered as struck out of flint, as rubbed from two sticks, as hiding in the wood, as hidden during the night in the waters, nay, judging from the warmth of the body, he was supposed to be dwelling even within us. Indra again was not simply the giver of rain, important as that primary function of his was in hot countries ; he was at the same time the wielder of the thunderbolt, the warrior fighting against the black clouds, the conqueror of their strongholds and deliverer of their prisoners. It was he who broke open their stable, and rescued the imprisoned cows, i. e. the waters of the clouds or the brio-lit dawns of the morning, the beautiful dawn-maidens. The more terrible the thunderclouds which he had to fight, the more powerful became the hero who could tear them all to pieces, and make them yield their hidden treasures, whether water or licdit. Ushas a^ain, the Dawn, was not only the bright light of the morning, she was the bringer of light and life, illuminating the whole sky, heralding the sun, flying before him, Il6 ORIGINAL ELEMENTS OF MYTHOLOGY. [CHAP.

and at last vanishing in the fiery embraces of her pursuer. There may be difference of opinion on the exact etymology of these divine names ; there can be none as to the fact that they all had an etymology, and that originally they all expressed the prominent actors in the never-ceasing: drama of nature.
Male and Female Agents.

Here we can see the first inevitable steps from mere agents to agents conceived as male and female. Agni and Indra would naturally remain male heroes, but the Dawn, originally ushas, and grammatically as yet neither masculine nor feminine, would as followed by the sun, as being loved by him, as seen fleeing before him, be naturally adorned with feminine epithets only. She would become an Ushas, or goddess, and serve as a type or example soon to be followed by other physical agents, such as the moon, the waters, or the earth, all frequently, though by no means always, conceived as female characters.
Common Epithets of Physical Agents.

From sharing some of their attributes in common, some of these unseen agents behind the veil of nature were soon spoken of by general names, whether as bright, i. e. deva, or as living, i. e. asura x, or as not ageing, i. e. a(/ara, or as never fading and dying, i. e. amnta, immortal. Sometimes the name of one of them would be extended to others, as, for instance, in Finnish, where Jumala is the name of the agent of the sky, or the thunder, but is after- 1 Asura is taken by Oldenberg as meaning possessed of miraculous power, wundermachtig ; why?

Il] COMMON EPITHETS OF PHYSICAL AGENTS. 117 wards extended to other deities also, so as to become in the end a name for gods in general. In Pali, Maru, originally a name of the storm-gods, has become a name for gods in general1. In Mongolian, also, tengri, originally the name of the sky and the god of the sky, comes afterwards to be used in the general sense of gods or spirits.

The Vedic Devas were to Dyaus what the Mongolian tengri were to Tengri. They were called immortal because they were always there. They had been known to fathers, grandfathers, and greatgrandfathers, and they would be known as the same by children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren. Who then was immortal, if they were not ? What are the Devas ? People speak so thoughtlessly, not to say foolishly, about the gods of various Aryan mythologies and their relation to each other, that it really seems necessary to remind them that not one of the gods ever possessed any substantive existence. There never was a Dyaus or a Mitra and Varmia, there never was a Zeus or a Jupiter. These gods are names in the fullest sense of the word, they are concepts or creations of the human brain, thus teaching us a lesson which is capable of much wider application. It is true that the conception of all the ancient Aryan gods was suggested by what we call real objects, by the great phenomena of nature, but they were fashioned as divine personalities by the mind of man (namarupa). Even such names as Agni, hre, Surya or Helios, sun, Ushas or Eos, dawn, though representing .the activities of real, of palpable or 1 S. B. E., vol. xxxii, p. xxiv.
zh: 人們幾乎難以理解，這類存在物（beings）如何竟能被容忍，除非我們承認，無論在希臘、印度或芬蘭，至少在較有教養的階層中，對其原初意義仍存有一絲模糊的記憶。

英雄或半神與諸神之間的關係，這個由格林、施瓦茨等人充分討論的問題，也容許另一種解答。必定存在這樣的情況：從一開始，這些神祇——尤其是太陽神祇——的事蹟，便以如此樸實且寫實的方式被講述，以致從最初起，其中的主要行動者就絕不可能被視為不朽者，而必然立即呈現出較不具神性、近乎凡人的性格，或者至少是僅略高於普通凡人尺度的存在。這些所謂的半神或英雄，例如赫拉克勒斯，往往與其神族親屬共享某些特定的稱號。他們常被稱為神聖的後裔……
> 父親與人類母親所生。他們甚至受到公認的崇拜，有時最終被允許加入眾神的行列。這再次說明瞭為何我們不必像格林那樣，將民間故事中的所有英雄都視為與其共享特質之神祇的墮落變體；我們至少可以接受其中一部分，是與諸神源自同一根源，只是從一開始便轉向了不同的發展途徑。
海倫娜既是女神又是英雄。
眾所周知，例如海倫娜（Helena）原是一位女神，在希臘擁有自己的神廟與崇拜。但這並不意味著這位女神後來變成了海倫（Helen）——無論是被忒修斯（Theseus）擄至阿菲德納（Aphidnae），還是被帕里斯（Paris）帶到特洛伊（Troy）。

這樣的過程難以理解，但一旦我們承認有一組傳說圍繞著海倫娜（Helena）之名而生——其中一些結合起來形成女神的形象，另一些則形成女英雄的形象——它便變得可以理解。在我們探究的這個階段，無論我們將海倫娜之名賦予「黎明」（Sarama）還是「月亮」（Selene）的原初意義，都無關緊要。只要我們想要解釋一位女神與一位女英雄海倫娜並存的事實，便足以記住：她那極其人性化的特質只能被匯聚在美麗的女英雄身上，而她超人的特質則使她適合成為神聖尊崇的對象——正如她在古希臘確實受到的崇敬¹。如此一來，施瓦茨（Schwartz）對格林（Grimm）體系提出的異議便可被重新審視¹。 ¹ Th. Heicks, *De Helena Dea*, Sigmaringen, 1863.
然而，諸神與英雄從相似的前因中同時起源的過程，必須得到充分的解釋。

哈恩在其重要著作《神話學研究》中已充分探討此問題。他在第五十一頁寫道：「我們不得不承認，最古老種族所產生的傳統，並未完整體現於我們所擁有的神話與英雄傳說之中。這些原始思想的相當部分保存至今，甚至儘管年代久遠，我們仍見其在民間思想中以不減的活力成長，並對其產生強烈反饋。童話的存在，以及同源民族間童話的明顯關聯，證明瞭這點。」在指出僅以一個種族向另一個種族借用童話的說法所面臨的困難後，哈恩博士證明，對這些民間故事進行仔細分析後，會發現其原始內容與構成神話英雄故事素材的自然現象相同，只是以更樸素的形式呈現。他認為，我們至今在雅利安語系不同分支中發現的這些童話起源，可追溯至雅利安人分離之前的時期。凡此種種，他或許完全正確；但當他拒絕考慮班費已用無可辯駁的證據證明的、歷史較晚的寓言從印度向歐洲的遷徙時，他便錯了。

質疑。如果我們移除所有已被證明是在歷史時期從印度一站一站傳入歐洲的東西——從梵語到巴列維語、到阿拉伯語、希臘語、希伯來語、拉丁語等等——那麼仍有大量民間傳說必須以其他方式來解釋。在我看來，本費的論據是無可反駁的，而哈恩所確立的結果也與本費的體系沒有任何衝突。這個過程，正如我們在英語中，除了大量共同的雅利安語詞彙之外，還發現一類在歷史時期從印度或波斯傳入不列顛群島、或從希臘語或拉丁語傳入哥德語的外來名稱一樣。這兩件事完全相容，甚至我們很少會對這些寓言究竟是共同雅利安遺產的一部分，還是後期輸入的產物感到困惑。

我們必須坦率承認，神話的真正起源已無可挽回地失落於人類史前時期的迷霧之中。當我們追溯至最古老的文獻——無論是吠陀（Veda）、奧義書（Upanishad），還是荷馬史詩——我們所見的神話體系早已發展完備，諸神之名已固定，其故事已成形。我們無法親眼見證「提婆」（Deva）如何從「特尤斯」（Dyaus）——那閃耀的天空之神——衍生而出；也無法目睹那將自然力量人格化的過程，如何從對「無限者」（the Infinite）的模糊直觀，逐步凝固為一個個具名的神祇。這一切，都發生在語言尚未凝固、思想仍在流動的遠古時代。因此，任何試圖重建神話起源的努力，都必然帶有推測的性質，如同透過深水觀看沉沒城市的殘垣斷壁。
我們永遠不應忘記，我們對古代民間傳統的認識實際上多麼有限——即便在印度人與希臘人的案例中亦然——而神話學最早的那些篇章，又已永遠失落於我們。對希臘人而言，荷馬史詩是他們神話、宗教乃至歷史最遙遠的背景；對我們來說，它卻是一幅繪飾精美的帷幕，必須先將它揭開，我們才有望看見神話戲劇最早的那些場景，或辨識出最初的演員，以及環繞他們的自然景物。
凱庫勒（Kekule）在其1877年出版的《神祇理想的起源》（Entstehung der Gotterideale）中的話語何其真切：他將希臘神話描述為僅僅是從人類以雙手層層堆疊的隱喻與夢境世界中所擷取的一個片段，或是從大自然那本偉大的圖畫書中撕下的一頁——對這本書而言，沒有什麼是過於渺小，也沒有什麼是過於崇高的。

「接著，」他繼續說道，「一旦這些圖像中的某一個對後代而言變得難以理解，淪為一個單純的名稱，便會從語言與詩歌的泉源中湧現出新的隱喻、新的形式、新的詩篇，用以再現同一自然現象的來去往復——直到那活水源頭幾近枯竭，卻從未完全乾涸，仍不斷湧出新的形象，只是不如從前那般強而有力；最終，自然本身那未被遺忘、雄渾原始的樂章與隱喻，所留下的唯有諸神與英雄的名號與形象，連同他們的故事，這些故事仍不斷萌發新的枝枒，綿延不絕。」

在謝林看來，神話並非單純的謬誤或欺騙，而是人類意識與神聖者之間一種必要且自然的發展過程。他將神話視為一種「神義論」（theodicy），亦即對神聖者如何在人類歷史中逐步顯現自身的辯證敘事。謝林區分了消極哲學（negative philosophy）與積極哲學（positive philosophy），前者處理純粹理性的範疇，後者則關注實存與歷史中的具體啟示。神話學（mythology）與天啟（revelation）皆屬於積極哲學的領域，前者是後者的預備階段。在神話中，人類尚未能把握唯一的、無限的上帝，而是透過多神教的形象，以一種必然的、有機的序列，逐步趨近對唯一真神的認識。這個過程並非任意編造，而是受制於一種內在的、精神性的法則。謝林因此反對將神話簡單歸結為自然現象的寓言式解讀，或視之為祭司欺騙的產物。他主張，神話是「真正的神話」（true mythology），其內容雖以多神形式呈現，卻承載著對唯一神聖力量的隱晦見證。這種觀點為後來比較神話學與宗教學的發展奠定了哲學基礎，尤其影響了穆勒等人對神話起源與宗教演進的探討。

那是謝林，我相信，是第一位抱怨「任何對荷馬的讚賞若未奠基於對其創作所遺留（überwunden）之遙遠過往的感知，便屬淺薄」的人。直到謝林逝世之後，藉由比較語言學與比較神話學，才得以在某種程度上揭開那道——正如他所清楚看見的——分隔荷馬當下與荷馬過往的簾幕。隨著每一年過去，我們愈來愈明白荷馬史詩究竟有多麼「現代」；我的意思是，它們預設了多少事物，又對宗教與神話民間傳說中豐富的累積有多少視而不見。如果《伊利亞德》只給我們特洛伊圍城戰的一個小片段，那麼《伊利亞德》與《奧德賽》合起來也只給我們一個更小的片段。
希臘民族在其眾多分支中廣泛散佈的神話、傳統、傳說與迷信，其龐大寶藏中的一部分。
而適用於希臘神話的道理，同樣適用於一切神話體系，甚至包括吠陀神話。儘管如此，我們有時卻能在吠陀中——比其他任何地方都更清楚——觀察到那始終先於真正神話誕生的醞釀過程本身。

在我們能夠理解神話的起源之前，必須先釐清一個根本問題：神話究竟從何而來？它並非某位詩人或祭司的刻意發明，而是人類心靈在面對**無限者**（the Infinite）時，必然產生的表達方式。當原始人試圖以語言捕捉那超越感官的實在，語言本身的侷限性便導致了神話的誕生——這正是我所謂的「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。 以雅利安（Aryan）民族的早期信仰為例，他們崇拜蒼穹之神**特尤斯**（Dyaus），這個詞在梵文中同時意指「照耀者」與「天空」。當後代人忘記這個詞的原始隱喻，便將**特尤斯**視為一個獨立的神祇，進而衍生出**提婆**（Deva）這類眾神。這種從自然現象到人格化神祇的轉變，正是**比較神話學**（comparative mythology）的核心課題。 值得注意的是，這種語言疾病並非隨機發生，而是遵循特定規律。在**梨俱吠陀**（Rig-Veda）中，我們看到一種被稱為**單一神教**（henotheism）或**輪換主神教**（kathenotheism）的現象：信徒在祈禱時，會將當下呼喚的神明視為至高無上的存在，但並不否認其他神明的地位。這種思維模式，正是從多神教（polytheism）過渡到一神教（monotheism）的關鍵環節。 因此，**宗教學**（the science of religion）必須與**語言科學**（the science of language）攜手合作。唯有透過比較雅利安、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）的神話體系，我們才能追溯人類宗教意識的演化軌跡。正如**奧義書**（Upanishad）所言：「彼即汝」（Tat tvam asi）——那無限者，始終隱藏在語言的面紗之後。
從對吠陀的研究，以及對其他種族古代神話殘跡的學術分析中，我們所獲得的認識，以及現今關於神話起源最普遍採納的結論，可歸納如下。

那些被稱為神靈的存在——無論是提婆（Devas）、θεοί、Dii 或 tivar——最初被召喚而出的過程，完全是自然而然的，甚至可以說是無可避免的。我們這些生活在語言（亦即思想）所累積的龐大財富中的人，能夠談論那些產生雷雨、閃電與降雨的自然力；但古人又能說些什麼呢？他們沒有詞語，也沒有思想，來表達我們意義中的「力」。或許，他們沒有這些詞語反倒是件幸事，因為我們自己對「力」的實質特性又能理解多少？容我再引一次 R. von Mayer 的話：它們並不比希臘的神靈高明多少。 如果古希臘人或印度的雅利安人（Aryas）開始追問：雨水與閃電從何而來？冰雹與雪、熱與冷、晝與夜——這些以規律或不規律的交替來去之物——從何而生？他們只能像談論那些耕田、鍛鐵或建屋的動作者與工作者一樣，來談論動作者與工作者。這不僅源於思想的必然性，同時也源於語言的必然性。如果他們想為風、火或太陽創造最初的名稱——用他們的語言所能產生的那種名稱——他們就必須使用與所有詞彙同源的相同詞根要素，也就是所謂的「根詞」：他們最早的謂詞、最早的抽象概念、最早的一般性術語。沒有一般性術語，就沒有名稱可言，除了像「咕咕」（cuckoo）或「汪汪」（bow-wow）這類模仿詞之外。 正如他們稱陶匠為「揉捏者」或「塑形者」（來自詞根 *dih*，意為揉捏、塑形），稱屠夫為「分割者」（*samitri*，來自 *sam*，用於準備或處理之意），他們也稱風為「吹拂者」——伐由（Vayu），來自 *va*，意為吹；稱太陽為「激發者」——薩維特爾（Savitṛ），來自 *su*，意為攪動；稱雲為「濕潤者」——梅伽（Megha），來自 *mih*，意為潤濕，或為「灑水者」——波爾闍尼耶（Parjanya），來自一個意為灑水的詞根，保留在拉丁文 *a-spergo* 中。透過創造這些名稱，他們創造了自己的提婆（Devas）；而這些提婆的「提婆性」（Devahood）——亦即其光明性，後來又成為神性——不過是他們物理活動的普遍補充罷了。

如果第一個物體的概念，如諾瓦雷（Noire）所示，源於對「已完成之工」（opus operatum）的意識——一個挖出的洞穴或一塊磨光的燧石——那麼因果概念便首次實現於對行為的意識中，即人類自身所運用的力量，以及在對提婆（Devas）——或我們所謂的自然力量——的認識中，這些正是神話學中的行為者名稱（nomina agentis）。參見穆勒（M. M.）《印度》等書，第227頁及以下。第一卷，I 114。神話學的原始元素。[第三章]

雅利安語根表達了動作，因此賦予自然偉大現象或活動的名稱，除了成為行為者名詞（nomina agentis）之外，別無可能。我無須再次探討語根的起源——可謂起源的起源——也無須再次解釋為何語根自然地表達動作，因為這些問題已在《思維科學》（Science of Thought）中詳盡論述。只需指出：迄今為止，無論從語音學或邏輯學角度試圖超越或分析這些語根的任何嘗試，都未能為神話學研究者帶來實質助益；儘管作為哲學問題，語根的起源將永遠對人類的好奇心施展其魅力。若說這些語根是情感性的，那無異於斷言我們感官的一切印象都是情感性的，且不可能有其他性質。

對我們而言，這些詞根卻是歷史紀念碑，比整個地球表面任何人造紀念碑都更為古老。作為詞根，它們絕不可能單獨存在，但若沒有它們，任何詞語也無法存在。對我們來說，它們當然是抽象概念，是從它們所出現的各種詞語中歸納出來的。然而，為了能夠出現在那些不同的詞語中，它們必須是某種真實且獨立的東西，就像絲線在被織成任何一種織物之前，必須先存在於織工手中一樣。當這些詞根被用來形成能夠涵蓋其下對象的名稱時，說雅利安語的人們便發現自己擁有了像阿耆尼（Agni）、因陀羅（Indra）、烏莎斯（Ushas）以及所有其他這類詞語。阿耆尼，即火，原意不過是敏捷者、快速移動者；婆訶尼（Vahni）意思大致相同，布羅尼尤（Bhuraṇyu）以及其他火的名稱也是如此。因陀羅意為濕潤者，烏莎斯意為照耀者。然而，除了表達這些一般性的活動之外，這些名稱還具有更明確的目的，它們會讓使用這些名稱的人聯想到許多事情，而不僅僅是其詞根所表達的簡單動作。對於那些創造了阿耆尼這個名稱的人們的心智與記憶而言，他不僅是顫動閃爍的光，或是每個家庭爐灶上溫暖與光明的來源；他也是吞噬森林者，是從雲層中迸發的致命閃電，是每日從海洋升起又再次沉入孕育了他的水域中的火球。人們記得他是從燧石中敲擊而出，是從兩根木棍中摩擦而生，是隱藏在木頭之中，是夜間潛藏於水域之內；不僅如此，根據身體的溫暖來判斷，他甚至被認為居住在我們體內。因陀羅同樣不僅僅是雨水的賜予者——儘管這項基本功能在炎熱的國度至關重要——他同時也是雷電的揮舞者，是與烏雲作戰的戰士，是攻克烏雲堡壘並釋放其囚徒的征服者。正是他衝破了它們的馬廄，救出了被囚禁的母牛，亦即雲中之水，或是清晨那被囚禁的黎明——美麗的黎明少女。他所必須對抗的雷雨雲越是可怕，這位能將它們全部撕碎、迫使它們交出隱藏寶藏（無論是水還是光）的英雄就變得越發強大。再來看烏莎斯，即黎明，她不僅是清晨的明亮光芒，她還是光明與生命的帶來者，照亮整個天空，預告太陽的來臨，在他面前飛馳。
最後，她在追逐者的火熱懷抱中消失。關於這些神聖名稱的確切詞源，或許存在意見分歧；但對於它們全都具有詞源，且最初皆表達了自然界永不停歇之戲劇中的主要角色這一事實，則無可爭議。
男性與女性的動力。

在此我們可見從單純的動因，到被設想為男性與女性的動因，其最初且無可避免的步驟。阿耆尼（Agni）與因陀羅（Indra）自然仍舊是男性英雄，但黎明（原為 ushas，文法上尚未有陰陽性之分）因跟隨太陽、為太陽所愛、在太陽之前逃逸，自然只會被賦予陰性的修飾語。她將成為烏莎斯（Ushas），即一位女神，並作為一種典型或範例，不久之後其他自然動因——如月亮、眾水或大地——也紛紛效法，雖然並非總是如此，但經常被設想為女性角色。
物質動因的常見別稱
¹ 奧爾登伯格（Oldenberg）將阿修羅（Asura）解釋為「擁有神奇力量者」（wundermächtig），其理由為何？
II] 自然力量的常見修飾語。117 此後亦擴展至其他神祇，最終成為眾神的通稱。在巴利語中，Maru 原為風暴之神的名稱，後來演變為眾神的通稱¹。在蒙古語中，tengri 原指天空及天空之神，其後亦被用來泛指諸神或靈體。

吠陀的提婆（Devas）之於特尤斯（Dyaus），正如蒙古的騰格里（tengri）之於騰格里（Tengri）。他們被稱為不朽，因為他們始終存在。他們為父輩、祖父輩與曾祖父輩所知曉，也將以同樣的面貌為子孫、孫輩與曾孫輩所認識。若他們並非不朽，那誰才是不朽的呢？提婆又是什麼？人們談論雅利安神話中諸神及其相互關係時，往往輕率得近乎愚蠢，以致確實有必要提醒他們：這些神祇中，從未有任何一位擁有實質性的存在。從來就沒有過特尤斯、密陀羅（Mitra）與伐樓那（Varuṇa），從來就沒有過宙斯（Zeus）或朱庇特（Jupiter）。這些神祇是這個詞最充分意義上的名稱，它們是人類頭腦中的概念或創造物，從而教導我們一個可以廣泛應用的道理。誠然，所有古代雅利安神祇的概念，都是由我們所謂的真實對象——即偉大的自然現象——所啟發的，但它們是由人類心智（nāmarūpa）塑造為神聖人格的。即便是阿耆尼（Agni）、火（hre）、蘇利耶（Surya）或赫利俄斯（Helios）、太陽、烏莎斯（Ushas）或厄俄斯（Eos）、黎明這些名稱，雖然代表著真實、可觸知或可感知的活動，¹ ¹ 參見《東方聖書》第三十二卷，第xxiv頁。
